coredata.dc:title: Oriented anchoring of NCQD on citric acid defective cluster of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) for the efficient removal of tetracycline via photo-Fenton catalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Jingyu
Zhang, Hanzhuo
Muhammad, Yaseen
Wei, Huijun
Wang, Ruimeng
Fang, Ge
Zhao, Zhongxing
Zhao, Zhenxia
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification and material composition are promising strategies for metal organic framework (MOF) to achieve excellent organic pollutant degradation performance. However, forming functional units inside MOF catalyst exactly at their active sites to improve charge dynamics remains challenges. Herein, oriented anchoring strategy was proposed to anchor nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with defective cluster of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) (NM8B) for highly efficient catalytic degradation tetracycline (TC) via synergistic photo-Fenton catalysis. The unique pyramidal charge transmission region connected by CNCO significantly promoted the charge kinetics from NCQDs to citric acid anchored NM8B, constructed Lewis acidic sites and strong ligand vacancies in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. The combination promoted the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, redox capability of Fe (II)/Fe (III) in MOF and enhanced the self-corrosion resistant stability. As a consequence, 93 % TC was oxidized (200 mg/L) in 30 min, showing ultra-fast degradation capacity of 527 mol⋅min−1⋅g−1, that rise 3–84 times higher than the reported renowned catalysts. In addition, the CG-QDs@C20-NM8B catalyst exhibited almost identical catalytic performance after 20 times reuse, exhibiting outstanding recycling performance than many reported MOFs. These intriguing merits rank CG-QDs@C20-NM8B as an efficient and promising choice for the degradation of TC from wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification and material composition are promising strategies for metal organic framework (MOF) to achieve excellent organic pollutant degradation performance. However, forming functional units inside MOF catalyst exactly at their active sites to improve charge dynamics remains challenges. Herein, oriented anchoring strategy was proposed to anchor nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with defective cluster of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) (NM8B) for highly efficient catalytic degradation tetracycline (TC) via synergistic photo-Fenton catalysis. The unique pyramidal charge transmission region connected by CNCO significantly promoted the charge kinetics from NCQDs to citric acid anchored NM8B, constructed Lewis acidic sites and strong ligand vacancies in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. The combination promoted the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, redox capability of Fe (II)/Fe (III) in MOF and enhanced the self-corrosion resistant stability. As a consequence, 93 % TC was oxidized (200 mg/L) in 30 min, showing ultra-fast degradation capacity of 527 mol⋅min−1⋅g−1, that rise 3–84 times higher than the reported renowned catalysts. In addition, the CG-QDs@C20-NM8B catalyst exhibited almost identical catalytic performance after 20 times reuse, exhibiting outstanding recycling performance than many reported MOFs. These intriguing merits rank CG-QDs@C20-NM8B as an efficient and promising choice for the degradation of TC from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is the second largest produced and used antibiotic for medical treatment and animal husbandry [1,2]. The harmful TC-contained water pollutants have caused the evolution of drug-resistant pathogenic strains in aquatic ecosystems and thus threaten human health [3–5]. To abate these impacts, diverse techniques have been employed for the treatment of TC polluted water. Among these techniques, coupled strategy of photo-Fenton catalysis via advanced oxidation has attracted substantial attention due to its high oxidative capacity, efficiency and environmental friendliness [6–8].

Currently, Fe-based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered suitable Fenton catalysts for the remediation of organic pollutants in sewage due to the presence of high density of dispersed Fe sites and ultra-high surface area providing abundant catalytic metal sites [9–12]. However, their limited Lewis acidity, modest charge separation and light absorption performance [13–15] result in rapid electron-hole recombination and low TC degradation rate. Thus, there is an urgent need for researchers to improve the TC degradation performance of Fe-based MOFs via photo-Fenton catalysis.

Defect engineering and heterojunction construction are typical and efficient strategies to solve these problems of Fe-MOF. For instance, Wang et al. used pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid to engineer defects in MIL-88B by in-situ synthesis, and the resultant defective mesopores and asymmetric metal coordination enhanced the mass diffusion and removal rate 7 times than the pristine MOF towards neonicotinoid insecticide [16]. Yu et al. reported defective MOF(Fe) catalysts synthesized by substituting Fe with Ce atoms, which exhibited 2 times higher photo-catalytic activity than that of the parent MIL-88A(Fe) [9]. In addition, constructing heterojunction can enhance the MOFs electron conductivity. Hu et al. proposed to combine MIL-88B(Fe) with conductive Bi2WO6 to construct Z-scheme photocatalyst, which could improve the photoexcited electron transport and separation from MIL-88B(Fe) to contaminants [17]. Li et al. proposed 2-D g-C3N4 to fabricate NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) hybrid which exhibited enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated h+ /e- which boosted the catalytic rate 2 ∼ 4 times compared to the pristine NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) [18]. These reported strategies can partially improve the electron conductivity of MOFs. However, their random hybridization could still not form efficient conductive channels between catalytic sites and organic contaminants. Therefore, designing specific positional combination between conductive materials and MOFs for improving the photo-Fenton catalytic performance is of great importance [19,20,21].

Herein, oriented anchoring strategy was proposed to form functional units with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) on defective cluster of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) (NM8B) as photo-Fenton catalyst for highly efficient degradation TC. In this system, NCQDs, as highly conductive nanodots [22–24], were anchored with NM8B via carboxyl-Fe cross linkage during NCQDs bottom-up growth in NM8B pores. Compared to other MOF composites, the most special combination mainly attributed to NCQDs oriented anchoring to defective Fe-O clusters and confined growth (CG) inside NM8B pores induced from Fe asymmetric ligand of citric acid (CA) via dual-solvent polymerization. In this way, the NCQDs was confined grown and unique pyramidal charge transmission region was formed within NM8B, which allows full play to its light capturing nano-harvester and separation capability of photoelectrons from vacancies. Thus, it can efficiently promote MOFs photo-Fenton catalytic activity for deep TC degradation. The engineered CG-QDs@MOFs (confined growth of NCQDs inside MOFs) were characterized by a series of techniques to elaborate the surface morphology, crystal structural, combination mode, and the corresponding photoelectric/redox reaction properties. Based on the of characterization and catalytic activity performance results, a plausible mechanism for the degradation of TC was proposed following photo-Fenton route.
Synthetic illustration of synthesis strategy for the above mentioned five NM8Bs is generalized in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine NM8B and CX-NM8B
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed description of original NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) is shown in supporting information and the prepared sample was labeled as NM8B.

Synthesis of CA modified NM8B (CX-NM8B): CX-NM8B was prepared via a similar procedure as that of NM8B except using extra 20 % (mole rate) of CA in NM8B precursor. The denoted sample was designated as CX-NM8B where X represent 5 %, 10 %, 20 % and 30 % with the corresponding samples as C5-NM8B, C10-NM8B, C20-NM8B and C30-NM8B respectively.

CG-QDs@C20-NM8B composites was prepared by duel-solvent method (DSM). The DSM method require two immiscible solvents: (1) solvent A could disperse MOF avoiding to resolve NCQD precursor; (2) solvent B could easily resolve NCQD precursor to introduce NCQD precursors into MOF pores without aggregation. After the reaction, it suffered the oxidation process at a relatively low temperature to synthesize NCQDs [25]. A certain amount of the synthesized C20-NM8B was dispersed in 30 mL n-hexane (as dispersed solvent A) under sonication for 30 min, which was marked as solution A. Then, the precursor of NCQDs (0.101 g urea and 0.081 g sodium citrate dihydrate with molar ratio of 6:1) was dissolved into 10 mL deionized water (solvent B) and the suspension was labeled as solution B. Subsequently, 0.2 mL solution B was dropped into solution A at 400 r/min for 12 h. Two different solvent was dispersed in each other and the surface between n-hexane and water could ensure the water resolved solution inside the hydrophilic MOF. The mixture was allowed to stand, and the supernatant was poured. After evaporating n-hexane completely, MOF solid powder was pyrolyzed at 180 °C using a Teflon-lined autoclave for 1.0 h to complete the oxidation process of confined growth of NCQDs inside C20-NM8B [25]. After the reaction, the dark brown solid was washed several times with deionized water and acetone. After drying, the corresponding nanohybrids were denoted as CG-QD@C20-NM8B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CG-QDs@NM8B and QDs@C20-NM8B composites
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison, CG-QDs@NM8B without CA anchor and QDs@C20-NM8B without dual-solvent confined strategy were synthesized. The procedure of preparation was identical to that of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B, except that C20-NM8B were replaced by original NM8B for CG-QDs@NM8B and C20-NM8B was impregnated with 0.2 mL solution B for QDs@C20-NM8B, respectively.
The detailed description of materials, synthesis methods and experiments are listed in Supporting Information.

Morphological characteristics and the surface charge properties of original NM8B and CA anchored NM8Bs were observed using SEM and Zeta potential. Original NM8B crystal in Fig. 1 a exhibited spindle shape with a length of 500–600 nm having a width of 30 to 100 nm and Zeta potential of about 4.05 mV. Its morphology and particle size were consistent with previous report [26,27]. Interestingly, with increasing the amount of CA in NM8B, the particle morphology of CX-NM8B gradually transformed from spindle-shape into cubic shape and their Zeta potential changed from 4.05 eV to −6.33 eV (Fig. 1b, Fig. S1) [28]. Among these, C20-NM8B displayed a uniform cubic shape with particle size about 300 nm, which indicate that CA can adjust the particle morphology and surface charge of NM8B [29,30]. However, excessive CA (30 % molar amount) in CX-NM8B may destroy the NM8B crystallinity. Thus, 20 % amount of CA was chosen to modify NM8B for further NCQDs confined growth. Fig. 1c showed a similar morphology of QDs@C20-NM8B to C20-NM8B with a Zeta potential of −13.47 eV due to the successful introduction of NCQDs (−12.9 mV) into NM8B. Moreover, these NCQDs did not change the morphology of MOFs. To further prove the existence of NCQDs in MOF, TEM and HRTEM were applied for CG-QDs@C20-NM8B and the results are shown in Fig. 1d. After the confined growth of NCQDs, some black shadow circles were observed at the edge of MOFs with 1–3 nm size. In order to observe NCQDs more clearly, NM8B was destroyed to expose more NCQDs (detail procedure in supporting information). After that, these exposed NCQDs were characterized via HRTEM. Fig. 1e and DLS number size distribution in Fig. S1h indicates that the residual NCQDs via bottom-up in-situ growth exhibited quasi-circular shape having a diameter of 1–3 nm and an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm [31]. The These results verified that NCQDs have been successfully grown in the pore of NM8B. On the contrary, CG-QDs@NM8B synthesized by the dual-solvent method without CA connection is shown in Fig. S1e, which suggests its similar morphology to parent NM8B having Zeta potential of −6.33 mV. This indicated that the NCQDs may be grown into the MOFs skeleton or the heating procedure of NCQDs change the surface charge property of NM8B [32]. However, the QDs@C20-NM8B composite (Fig. S1f) showed clear grain deformation having NCQDs outside surface of NM8B, indicating non-uniform distribution of NCQDs with NM8B. To show the variation of Zeta potential clearly, the data were shown as Fig. S1g.

Fig. 2 a shows the PXRD patterns of NM8Bs, which suggests that pristine NM8B exhibited typical diffraction peaks positioned at 10.2° and 9.2° belonged to (101) and (002) crystallites planes of the pristine NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) [33]. After the addition of CA, C20-NM8B exhibited similar diffraction peaks except the slight right shift of (101) facet peak and the increasing peak intensity of (002) facet (Fig. S2). This indicated the implantation of CA slightly contracted the lattice of NM8B crystals. Thus, in-situ addition of CA showed influence on lattice contraction and (002) crystal regulation of CX-NM8B [34]. However, after overdoping of CA, C30-NM8B exhibited wide range peaks belonging to amorphous carbon polymer, indicating the damage NM8B crystal structure [35]. Among the samples, C20-NM8B displayed the most intensified (002) facet, and thus 20 % amount of CA was chosen for further step of NCQDs confined growth. After the confined growth of NCQDs, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B showed further intensification of (002) facet. Meanwhile, (101) plane at 2θ = 10.3° shifted to higher value which was caused by the contraction of the crystal lattice from exerted stress via implanted NCQDs interstitial-atom in C20-NM8B pores [36]. As compared, CG-QDs@NM8B and QDs@C20-NM8B both have no peak shifting of (002) facet. The NCQDs of the former has no connection with NM8B since they have no CA as anchor point, while for the latter, the precursor of NCQDs can hardly disperse inside the chanel of NM8B, leading to the unchanged crystalite stucture of C20-NM8B.

To investigate the effect of anchored CA and NCQDs on the stability of MOF structure, TG analysis were applied on NM8Bs under N2 and shown in Fig. 2b. As seen, the DTG profiles of pristine NM8B exhibited three peaks at 70, 284 and 474 °C, which represented H2O desorption, chemically bonded H2O and structure decomposition, respectively [37]. By comparison, C20-NM8B exhibited much higher H2O desorption peak at 101 °C, indicating the appearance of more adsorbed sites and high affinity for H2O [38]. CA implantation generated portion of asymmetric coordination and resulted in the increase of unsaturated metal sites, which increased adsorption affinity and adsorbed amount of H2O on C20-NM8B. This provided hydrophilic microenvironment in MOF pores, which would provide hydrophilic anchor to attract more NCQDs precursors for the dual-solvent method and lead to the tight growth of NCQDs in MOFs pores [39]. After the growth of NCQDs in C20-NM8B, H2O adsorption peak of CG-QD@C20-NM8B was shifted to lower temperature (67 °C) with weak intensity. This indicated that the subsequent growth of NCQDs in CG-QD@C20-NM8B pores led to the decrease of surface area and surface hydrophobicity of MOF [40], and thus confirm the directional anchor process by the occupied adsorption sites and affinity towards H2O. Moreover, these confined growth of NCQDs enhanced the combination interaction of H2O chemical bonding (FeOH and the chemical bonding water in NCQDs) with CG-QDs@C20-NM8B, which resulted in increasing its temperature desorption peak from 284 to 297 °C. Furthermore, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B (52 %) displayed higher amount of residual ratio than NM8B (47 %), confirming the existence of NCQDs in NM8B frameworks.

By comparison, CG-QDs@NM8B having confined NCQDs without CA bridging showed similar DTG profile to parent NM8B, indicating very weak interaction between the grown NCQDs and NM8B [41]. For non-confined QDs@C20-NM8B, the three weight loss peaks shifted to the left and the rest component (44 %) was less than NM8B, suggesting its weak crystal stability compared to CG-QDs@C20-NM8B [42]. This indicated that the dual-solvent strategy could protect MOF structure, and thus maintained thermal stability during confined growth in CA linked NM8B.

Fig. 2c shows the N2 adsorption isotherms and their calculated textural parameters for NM8B and its NCQDs composites are listed in Table 1 . As seen, all of these samples exhibited type III N2 sorption isotherms with relatively low BET surface areas (S BET). NM8B exhibited enclosed pores in dry state due to their flexible breathing nature [43,44]. As the porous texture of these samples is hard to be measured under liquid conditions (2815 m2/g as reported [45]), thus, their porous property in dry state was chosen to investigate the effect of NCQDs on NM8B. As seen, C20-NM8B showed lower S BET (28.32 m2/g) compared to pure NM8B (40.9 m2/g), which is mainly attributed to the generated defective pores from the substitution of CA for ATA (2-amino-terephthalic acid) ligand [46,47]. After the confined growth of NCQDs, a dramatic decrease in S BET (15.9 m2/g) was observed in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. However, its microporous surface area was clearly higher than that of C20-NM8B. This may be ascribed to the generation of new micropores due to the partial occupation of NCQDs inside the mesopores of C20-NM8B [48]. The S BET of CG-QDs@NM8B showed almost no decrease compared to that of the parent NM8B, indicating less NCQDs in the pore of MOF than CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. On the other hand, QDs@C20-NM8B showed increased S BET compared to C20-NM8B (Table 1). This phenomenon inferred that these unconfined NCQDs displayed the porous property due to their large 3-D size [49].

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2d shows that all the samples exhibited the characteristic bands of NM8B. As seen, two peaks positioned at 3300 and 3475 cm−1 belonged to the vibration of the NH of –NH2, and the vibration bands at position of about 3000, 1600, 765 and 1255 cm−1 were due to the characteristic vibrations of CH, CC and the NC bending vibration in benzene rings from organic ligands of NM8B, respectively [50]. After CA modification, the peak of –COOH group (1577 cm−1) was intensified which indicated the increasing amount of –COOH in C20-NM8B [51]. Furthermore, CG-QDs@NM8B and CG-QDs@C20-NM8B via dual-solvent method showed the same spectra as NM8B and C20-NM8B. Almost no typical chemical groups from NCQDs were detected in FT-IR spectrum of CG-QDs@NM8B and CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. However, they projected two different cases. For CG-QDs@NM8B, its pore texture exhibited very similar to that of NM8B. Combined with its corresponding FT-IR spectrum, it indicated that almost no NCQDs grew on or within NM8B. For CG-QDs@C20-NM8B, NCQDs were observed from its HRTEM and the pore volume decreased compared to C20-NM8B. Combined with its corresponding FT-IR spectrum, it indicated that NCQDs grew not on but within C20-NM8B. On the contrary, QDs@C20-NM8B without confined growth exhibits much higher intensity of NH bond at 3350 cm−1 compared to C20-NM8B, referring large amount of exposed NCQDs on C20-NM8B surface [52,53]. This confirmed the successful confined growth of NCQD inside NM8B.

XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) spectra and elemental components analysis of NM8B and its composites are shown in Fig. 3 , Fig. S3 and Table S1 to investigate the effects of CA modification and the confined growth of NCQDs in NM8B. As shown in the Table S2, the atomic contents of C, N and Fe in the original NM8B are 58.39 at.%, 7.47 at.% and 4.66 at.%, respectively [33]. It is worth noting that the ratio of Fe/O element in C20-NM8B (0.190) increased compared to NM8B (0.158) because CA increased the defective site by the competitive coordination to Fe(III) with its three –COOH groups. After the directional growth of NCQDs, the C and N content of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B increased slightly, indicating the uniform and successful loading of NCQDs. Combined with our TG analysis, NCQDs could be verified to grow into porous cages of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B via dual-solvent approach rather than covering crystal surface of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B. As compared, the C and N atom content of CG-QDs@NM8B and QDs@C20-NM8B without directional anchor or confined strategy was increased. This suggested that NCQDs were hardly grown in the channel of MOFs without anchoring CA [54]. Besides, NCQDs preferred to gather on the surface of MOF crystals rather than growing inside of pores without confined strategy.

Fig. 3a presents the high resolution Fe 2p XPS spectra of NM8B samples. The original NM8B was decomposed into four energy level peaks located at binding energy (BE) of 711.8, 725.5, 716.3 and 730.6 eV, assigned to Fe(III) (2p 3/2), Fe(III) (2p 1/2) and two satellite peaks, respectively [55]. In C20-NM8B, the BE of Fe decreased by 0.3 eV, which suggests that the addition of CA generated defective Fe-O clusters, and thus rise the electron density of Fe (III) compared to pure Fe (III) coordination with carboxyl groups from ATA ligands [56]. After the confined growth of NCQDs, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited two new peaks belonged to Fe(II) peaks appearing at 708.6 and 722.3 eV. When NCQDs was grown inside C20-NM8B, the high π electron density from NCQDs transferred electrons and reduced ferric ions to Fe(II) through CA brigding. This Fe(II) in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B can enhance photo-Fenton catalytic activity by promoting Fe(II)/Fe(III) transition [57]. As compared, the Fe 2p spectra of CG-QDs@NM8B and QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited similar BE to that of NM8B and C20-NM8B due to the absence of bridging between NCQDs and NM8B. Therefore, only confined growth of NCQDs in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B connected with CA could affect Fe coordination and generate multivalence Fe in C20-NM8B [44].

Fig. 3b indicates the full survey XPS spectra of N 1s in NM8B composites. The N 1s spectra of original NM8B can be decomposed into two energy level peaks located at BE of 399.5 and 400.6 eV, attributed to NH and Ar-N, respectively [58]. Moreover, the peak area of Ar-N/NH was about 0.35, which was similar to that of NM8B ligand [59]. After CA implantation in NM8B, the content of Ar-N/NH remained unchanged compared to NM8B, negating the occurrence of reaction between CA and amino groups in ATA. As shown, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B presented two new peaks of N 1s spectrum representing the OCNC at 398.7 and NO at 402.2 eV, respectively. Also, its peak area ratio of Ar-N/NH showed a significant increase from 0.35 to 0.60. These results indicated that when NCQDs grew in the MOFs pore, –NH2 groups from urea and MOF ligand participated in the polymerization reaction of NCQDs as the end-capping agent[60], resulting in increasing content of OCNC but a significant decrease in the content of NH. In contrast, CG-QDs@NM8B detected no OCNC bond, indicating no anchored NCQDs in NM8B were hard nucleation of dispersed NCQD precursors. Thus, only the slight increase of NH bond and the appearance of NO peak represented the existence of NCQDs. For unconfined QDs@C20-NM8B, OCNC content was significantly higher than that of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B due to the aggregation of NCQDs on the surface of NM8B. In addition, the peak intensity ratio of AR-N/NH remained almost unchanged compared with C20-NM8B (0.36), indicating that there was almost no amination reaction between the unconfined NCQDs and the NH2 from ATA. This analysis indicated that directional anchoring and confined growth of NCQDs in the pore of MOF showed high dispersion in pore cage of MOFs, which was verified to connect with the carboxyl group of CA and the –NH2 of NM8B ligand. This would form the unique pyramidal charge transmission region which can significantly improve the photoelectron transfer through OCNC connection through this functional units. As the hub of electron conduction, NCQDs connect the defective ligand CA and the original ligand NH2-BDC. The number of connections was random, but the locations of the connections were concentrated around the defective ferric oxygen clusters.

Before the photo-Fenton catalytic degradation, the dark adsorption study on these photo-Fenton catalysts was conducted, and the results are shown in Fig. S4a. It can be seen that all these five catalysts exhibited low adsorption ratio towards TC due to low amount of catalyst dosage. The calculated adsorption capacity for these catalysts is shown in Fig. S4b. As expected, the adsorbed uptake decreased after the NCQDs growth in NM8B, which is attributed to the decrease of surface area and pore volume in NCQDs grown NM8B [42].

Also, the control experiments for under different conditions were conducted (Fig. S4c). When using Vis, H2O2, Vis/H2O2 or Vis/NM8B as degradation conditions, the degradation curves of TC were almost straight lines having C/C 0 = 1, indicating almost no catalytic degradation. The degradation ratios of TC without H2O2, were 3 ∼ 6 % within 60 min, while a similar scenario was found the experiment performed without light irradiation or catalysts.

Fig. 4 compiles the TC degradation using different photocatalysts under different reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. 4(a and b), the original NM8B showed a very slow degradation rate in the initial of 30 min of reaction. After CA modification, the degradation of TC was significantly accelerated, reaching to 99 % after 75 min, and C20-NM8B achieved 99 % removal of TC after 60 min. Furthermore, C20-NM8B orientated anchored NCQDs exhibited even faster degradation rate for TC, showing that t 90 (the time that is required to reach 90 % of the total degradation) was only within 30 min. In addition, the inadequate contact between NCQDs and MOFs in CG-QDs@NM8B and QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited relatively lower catalytic activity with slower rate compared to C20-NM8B. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (1)) evaluating degradation kinetics in Fig. 4b indicated an order of TC degradation rate of: CG-QDs@C20-NM8B (0.116 min−1) > C20-NM8B (0.069 min−1) > CG-QDs@NM8B (0.033 min−1) > QDs@C20-NM8B (0.029 min−1) > NM8B (0.024 min−1). Among which, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited 4.8 times enhanced degradation kinetics than original NM8B. Clearly, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B having oriented anchor of NCQDs with NM8B via CA showed the highest degradation kinetics. This is because CA modification engineered more unsaturated Fe sites in NM8B through asymmetric coordination from three –COOH groups in CA, leading large amount of defective Fe-O clusters, which are of great importance in the photo-Fenton reaction. For CG-QDs@C20-NM8B, electron-rich NCQDs was proved to anchor onto NM8B via –COOH of CA and –NH2 of NM8B ligand under space-confined conditions, which was directly linked with highly active position of defective Fe-O clusters [61]. As supposed, MOF ligands having rich NH groups became the light nano-harvester and introduced photoelectrons in MOF cages. Then, with the aid of the electron-rich NCQDs, the unique pyramidal charge transmission region carried photoelectrons to defective Fe-O clusters and synergistically promoted NM8B photo-Fenton catalytic performance [62].

= k t

The effect of CA modification amount and NCQDs growth amount on the degradation of TC is provided in Fig. 4(c and d). As shown, the photo-Fenton TC degradation rate slightly increased with the increasing the doping amount of CA from 5 % to 30 %, while it decreased with doping further CA. Excessive CA amount could lead to collapse of MOF skeleton and formation of more structural defects [21], which eventually damaged MOF photo-Fenton catalytic performance. Moreover, the effect of NCQDs growth amount (from 4.5 to 10) on TC degradation performance using CG-QDs@C20-NM8B is shown in Fig. 4d. Obviously, TC degradation rate gradually increased with the increase of anchored NCQDs till 200 μL NCQDs precursors and decreased with further addition of NCQDs [63]. This shows that only properly anchored NCQDs in NM8B would facilitate photo-Fenton catalytic performance, and their excessive filling would block MOF pores and cover its catalytic sites.

Fig. 4 (e and f) show the effect of concentrations of TC and H2O2 on the photo-Fenton degradation of TC. TC degradation rate on CG-QDs@C20-NM8B decreased and increased with increasing TC and H2O2 concentration, respectively. Among the various NM8B catalysts, the most suitable dosages of H2O2 and TC concentration in system were 2 mM and 200 mg/L, respectively. The poor conductivity of MOFs would lead to the self-corrosion by photogenerated electrons, resulting in the destruction of catalyst [64], hence the catalytic structure stability of MOFs composites is an important property for catalytic activity evaluation of MOFs.

Recycling performance of catalyst plays key role in managing process cost [65]. In order to evaluate the chemical stability of the catalyst, cyclic degradation experiments and the corresponding Fe3+ leakage were carried out on CG-QDs@C20-NM8B under the same catalytic conditions (Fig. 5 a). After 20th successive recycling reaction, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B experienced almost no decline in TC degradation kinetics. Interestingly, after the 20th cycle, only 0.3 wt% leaked Fe3+ was found under neutral condition, suggesting the intriguing high cycling stability than many renowned MOFs [15,66]. Furthermore, XRD tests were carried out for the materials after each cycle and shown in Fig. 5b, which indicate consistent peak pattern with that of fresh catalyst, indicating their excellent structural stability against electron or free radical attack during photo-Fenton degradation. For CG-QDs@C20-NM8B, CA modification and NCQDs oriented anchor synergistically promoted Fe3+/Fe2+ transition and transfer rate of photo-generated electrons, thus suppressed MOF self-corrosion from its generated carriers under visible light irradiation and surrounding reactive radicals.

The catalytic efficiency of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B for TC degradation was compared with many reported state-of-the-art catalysts and the results are listed in Table S2. CG-QDs@C20-NM8B can degrade high concentration of TC at lower catalyst dosage and H2O2 concentration with 3–29 times higher catalytic kinetic constant K than those of the reported catalysts. Thus, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B with ultra-high photo-Fenton degradation capacity for TC and excellent chemical stability exhibits great potentials for large-scale removal of TC from wastewater.

The optical properties and electrochemical activities of photo-Fenton catalysts are of great importance for the photo-Fenton catalytic performance [67,68]. Fig. 6 a showed UV–vis DRS (UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy) spectra of various NM8Bs, which suggests that pure NM8B exhibited a relatively broad absorption peak in a range of 300–800 nm with the peak position at 300 and 550 nm. After CA modification, C20-NM8B showed enhanced light absorption in visible region from wavelength of 400–550 nm, but displayed a decrease in the range of 550–800 nm. This was because of the introduction of more –COOH groups instead of –NH2 into NM8B during a portion substitution of CA for ATA ligand [69]. When NCQDs were oriented anchored within NM8B via CA, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited a dramatic enhancement in light absorption in visible region (400–600 nm) compared to C20-NM8B. Clearly, the formed NCQDs in NM8B worked as the light nano-harvester, and facilitated light absorption during photo-Fenton reaction. On the contrary, the light absorption of CG-QD@NM8B was clearly lower than that of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B due to the absence of chemical connection between NCQDs and NM8B. Lower number of transfer channels for the optical energy due to the interfacial energy barrier between NCQDs and NM8B [20] lead to lower catalytic activity of CG-QDs@NM8B. While for unconfined grown QDs@C20-NM8B, the aggregation of plenty of NCQDs particles on NM8B crystals reduced the exposure of light-absorbing group and thus damaged its light absorption ability [70].

Furthermore, photo-generated electron-hole separation properties in Fig. 6b were detected from PL (Photoluminescence) spectra at an excitation wavelength of 387 nm where a weaker PL signal indicates higher separation rate of photogenerated e-/h+ pairs [71]. Pure NM8B exhibited the strongest PL peak intensity demonstrating high rate of recombination of the e-/h+ pairs under light irradiation. After CA modification of the NM8B, this intensity dramatically decreased, indicating the promotion of transfer rate of photo-generated electrons on defective Fe-O clusters. Moreover, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited a further decrease of PL peak intensity having oriented anchored NCQDs within C20-NM8B, implying the improvement of separation efficiency of photogenerated carrier benefitted from the pyramidal charge transmission region between NCQDs and MOF.

The electron transfer and Fe (II)/Fe (III) redox abilities of the catalysts were measured by EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy) (Fig. 6c) and CV (Cyclic Voltammetry) (Fig. 6d). The order of R ct values of these NM8Bs was: NM8B (242.7 Ω) > C20-NM8B (152.2 Ω) > QDs@C20-NM8B (134.1 Ω) > CG-QDs@NM8B (95.1 Ω) > CG-QDs@C20-NM8B (21.8 Ω). It is observed that the introduction of CA decreased R ct value because it increased quantity of active sites. Clearly, the growth of NCQDs would improve the electronic conductivity of NM8Bs, and thus reduced the corresponding R ct value. As seen, R ct value of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B was reduced about 10 times of original NM8B, which was due to the OCNC bond between g-CNQD and CA anchored NM8B. This greatly reduced the electron transport barrier between NM8B and NCQDs [36,72]. By comparison, the oriented anchored NCQDs within C20-NM8B showed a dramatic decrease of R ct from 152.0 to 21.8 Ω. This proved that the connection position of NCQDs in NM8B has great influence on electronic conductivity and transfer of MOFs. In case of these materials, the connection position of defective Fe-O cluster was proved to significantly enhance charge separation for NM8B [73].

Electrochemical test cycle volt-ampere curve in Fig. 6d was used to explore the electron transfer ability of the prepared materials and Fe (II)/Fe (III) redox ability by ΔE p (difference of the redox potential) and I p (I pa and I pc indicates the oxidative and reductive peak currents, respectively). As shown, NM8B possessed the maximum ΔE p and the minimum I p values demonstrating the worst oxidation or reduction capacity and lowest separation efficiency of e-/h+ pairs, respectively. On the contrary, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B possessed the maximum ΔE p value and the minimum I p value, indicating that the high-density electron cloud on NCQDs can participate in NM8B through the bond formed by CA. Thus, the pyramidal charge transmission region could efficiently accelerate the conversion between Fe2+ and Fe3+ and enhance photocatalytic activity of NM8B [74].

Mott-Schottky measurements were performed on these materials to study the charge carrier density at the interface between the semiconductor electrode and electrolyte, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. As seen, tangent lines to the curves showed positive slopes for all five samples, indicating that these materials belonged to N-type semiconductors [70]. The lower slope of C20-NM8B (5.3) and CG-QDs@C20-NM8B (3.1) represented for the higher carrier density than NM8B (18.6), CG-QDs@NM8B (14.7) and QDs@C20-NM8B (17.1). This is attributed to the intimate interface and more active sites with the addition of CA/NCQDs on the Fe-O clusters in MOF [42]. The oriented anchored NCQDs can significantly promote the formation of space charge layer, thus obtaining better photocatalytic performance [56].

Fig. 7b included both Mott-Schottky curves and VB XPS spectra of C20-NM8B. The data were converted to standard hydrogen potentials using Eq. (2).

The conduction band potential (E CB) of C20-NM8B was calculated to be −0.75 eV while its valence band (VB) (1.22 eV) was measured by the VB XPS. The photo-generated electron was first quickly transferred from the CB of NM8B to NCQDs via the OCNC bond of pyramidal charge transmission region, which was used to reduce the surface oxygen molecules to O2‾ radicals on the photocatalyst intimate surface. This was consistent with the results of ESR experiment. The photo-generated hole participated in the oxidation of TC directly at VB of NM8B. The band gap was calculated according to Eq. Where E g, E VB and E CB are energy gap, valence band energy and conduction band energy, eV, respectively.

Various trapping agents were applied to test the active species involved in photo-Fenton TC degradation. In Fig. 7c, TC degradation resulted in OH, O2−, h + and e - (illuminated by 1.0 mmol EDTA-2Na, BQ, TBA and AgNO3), respectively [75]. After scavenging O2, OH−, h + and e -, the TD degradation got to 65.6, 70.1 and 80.6 % and 96.6 % for TC. Thus, O2– and OH with strong oxidation ability were proved to be the primary reactive radicals during the TC degradation.

Subsequently, the ability of NM8B and CG-QDs@C20-NM8B to create O2− and OH under both dark condition and visible light irradiation were detected by using ESR [76]. In the absence of UV light irradiation, no peaks DMPO- OH and O2− appeared within 10 min (Fig. S5), suggesting that no O2− and OH were generated, while such peaks appeared on CG-QDs@C20-NM8B (Fig. 7d). For these peaks, the intensity of OH and DMPO- O2− was 1.45 and 1.72 times higher than that of NM8B under visible light irradiation, respectively. These findings were in accordance with the those of free radical capturing experiment (Fig. 7c).

The CUSs amount in MOFs cluster demonstrates the Lewis acidity of the catalysts. The CUSs amount in the photocatalysts was determined using CO in-situ FTIR and the results are shown in Fig. 7e. The absorption peaks at 2169 and 2116 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO chemisorbed at the corresponding Fe CUSs over NM8B and CG-QDs@C20-NM8B [77]. The peak intensity of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B was much stronger than that of NM8B, suggesting significantly enriched ligand vacancies and Lewis acidic sites presumably appeared due to the increased Fe CUSs content. Moreover, their intensity decreased with the corresponding desorption temperature, showing the weaker binding force of CO on Fe CUSs from NM8Bs. When the desorption temperature reached 423 K, the intensity of these two peaks for CG-QDs@C20-NM8B still remained higher compared to that of NM8B at 303 K. This demonstrated the stronger Lewis acidities of these Fe CUSs in CG-QDs@C20-NM8B [16,44].

On the basis of this discussion, O2− and OH were demonstrated to be the main active radicals in this TC degradation process. The high catalytic activity of CG-QDs@C20-NM8B was attributed to the defect engineering of CA, directional growth of NCQDs and the pyramidal charge transmission region. Dispersed CG-QDs in C20-NM8B can efficiently promote light usability and enhance MOFs photoelectric transfer efficiency. Hence, the photo-generated electrons redox of Fe(III) cluster was enhanced up (Eq. (4)) [74], and then triggered photo-Fenton process Eq. (5 and 6) to produce OH (Fig. 7f). Fe (II)/Fe (III) redox Eq. (4) is the determining step of reaction rate in the catalytic system involved photo-Fenton process [78]. The pyramidal charge transmission region directional anchored NCQDs on defective Fe cluster could easily transfer light generated e - that used for both MOF Fe (II)/Fe (III) transfer and production of O2– from dissolved water Eq. (7).
(4) Fe3+ + e− → Fe2+
(5) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3++ OH + OH−
(6) Fe3+ + H2O2 → Fe2++ OOH + H+
(7) O2 + e− → O2 −
(8)
OH/O2 −/h+ + TC → several steps → CO2 + H2O

The TC degradation reaction intermediates and four possible pathways were identified by LC-MS using CG-QDs@C20-NM8B/TC and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . A schematic of TC degradation based on the previous analysis was illustrated. TC has three types of functional groups including double bond, phenolic group, and amine group, which can be easily attacked by OH and O2– because of their relatively high electron densities [79]. In path one, under the attack of OH, intermediates TP 461 were generated by hydroxylation [80]. Subsequently, TP 437 turned to small molecular weight intermediates, such as TP 301, TP 274, TP 149, TP 116 and TP 60, which were produced by further reactions around anime groups. This means that amine groups, due to their high electron density, are vulnerable to attack by free radicals.

TP 417 in path two was the product of TC after dehydration. It can be decomposed via two pathways. Subsequently, intermediate was produced by demethylation, dihydroxylation and ring-opening. It lost dimethyl amino and dihydroxyl to form TP 360, creating a ketone on the phenolic ring. After dihydroxylation, TP 360 was further oxidized by free radicals and non-free radicals with small molecular weights.

In path three and four, TC was degraded into TP 425 and TP 443 via demethylation and dehydrogenation reaction, respectively. Then, the methyl functional groups connected to N were fractured from the skeleton and converted to TP 415 and TP 403 [81]. After several demethylation and dihydroxylation, it can also turn to TP 274 from the second pathway. The specific MS spectra of the intermediates are listed in Fig. S7. Finally, a part of these pathways would finally lead to CO2 and H2O, which was confirmed by TOC (Fig. S6). The TC mineralization rate by CG-QDs@C20-NM8B/TC reached 80 % within 6 h of the reaction. These results concluded that CG-QDs@C20-NM8B can effectively convert the obnoxious TC into toxicologically benign and harmless products with minimal health hazards.

In summary, oriented anchoring strategy was proposed to anchor nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with defective cluster of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) for enhancing synergistic photo-Fenton catalytic degradation of TC. Effects of CA modification and growth mode of NCQDs on NM8B on CG-QDs@C20-NM8B structure construction and their catalytic performances for TC were evaluated. Characterization measurements showed that CA implantation generated portion of asymmetric coordination on MOF skeleton to form active defective cluster showing strengthened Fe Lewis sites. NCQDs were introduced to grow into NM8B confined framework via intimate CNCO interface as functional units, and finally constructed the unique pyramidal charge transmission region of CA/ligand-Fe (III/II)-NCQDs. This enhanced the photo-energy harvesting at CA/ligand, accelerated electron-hole pair separation at NCQDs and long-lived trapped electrons at defective Fe (III/II) clusters. As a result, CG-QDs@C20-NM8B exhibited excellent degradation performance of t90 = 30 min, 80.1 % TOC within 6 h and 3–84 times higher catalytic capacity for TC degradation compared with other novel catalysts. After recycling for 20 consecutive cycles, the CG-QDs@C20-NM8B demonstrated low loss photo-Fenton catalytic activity. Hence, the oriented anchoring strategy for the preparation CG-QDs@C20-NM8B via fabricating NCQDs into MOFs pores can be deemed of ideal catalysis for the photo-Fenton catalytic degradation of TC in wastewater.
